Wigan Warriors entered the season following their sixth-placed league finish and subsequent play-off knock-out in the [[Super League XIV|2009 Super League season]], as well as their semi-final knock-out in the [[2009 Challenge Cup]]. The 2010 season is the eleventh year in which [[rugby league]] has been played at the [[Wigan|town]]'s [[DW Stadium]]. [[Michael Maguire (rugby league)|Michael Maguire]] started in his position as the new head coach of the club during this season, following [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]]'s departure. It is also the first season for [[Paul Deacon]], who signed for the club in November 2009. It was the first season in which the captaincy of the team was allocated through a newly formed leadership team of five players (later expanded to six players). The season covers the period between 1 November 2009 and 31 October 2010. following three pre-season friendlies, the season started officially with the 38–6 victory over [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders RL]] at the DW Stadium in the [[Super League XV]] round 1 fixture on Friday 5 February 2010. ==Background== {{see also|History of Wigan Warriors}} Wigan Warriors' [[2009 Challenge Cup]] campaign ended at the penultimate stage after they lost to [[Warrington Wolves]] in the semi-final at [[Widnes]]' [[Halton Stadium|Stobart Stadium Halton]].{{cite For Wigan Warriors, this was against [[Huddersfield Giants]]. Finishing the regular season in the top eight places means entering the [[Super League play-offs|play-offs]] for those teams concerned, potentially an additional four matches, including the [[Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]] itself. The club will also compete in the [[2010 Challenge Cup]], entering in the fourth round. ===February=== An [[Amos Roberts]] brace gave Wigan their first win of the season in the opening [[Round robin tournament|round]] against [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders RL]], who were led by the Warriors' former head coach [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]].{{Cite The win meant that Wigan ended the regular season with twenty-two wins and five losses, beating their nearest rivals in St Helens and Warrington to the top of the table by four points.{{cite web|title=League Table|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/table10|work=Super League|publisher=Rugby Football League|accessdate=16 July 2011}} By virtue of this, they would play fourth-placed Leeds in the first weekend of the playoffs. A drop-goal from [[Kevin Sinfield]] proved the difference as Wigan lost their first playoff match in controversial circumstances, as in the final minute of the game, McGuire took out Carmont and [[Scott Donald]] took out Richards as Wigan were breaking down the left wing, foul play which injured McGuire's knee and ruled him out for the rest of the playoffs, and gave Wigan a penalty from forty metres out which would have won the game had Richards not missed it.{{cite news|title=Wigan Warriors 26–27 Leeds Rhinos|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/8980912.stm|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=BBC|date=12 September 2010}}{{cite news|title=Leeds Rhinos' Danny McGuire ruled out of play-offs|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/leeds/8995569.stm|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=BBC|date=13 September 2010}} The consequence of defeat by Leeds was a match against Hull Kingston Rovers, with the Warriors missing Deacon due to a groin injury received during the previous match.{{cite news|title=Deacon loses fitness race|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12217_6378450,00.html|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=15 September 2010}} However, despite being behind twice during the match at 6–12 and 12–14, Wigan eventually overcame Hull Kingston Rovers 42–18 to place themselves into the playoff semi-finals.{{Cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8995523.stm|title = Wigan 42-18 Hull KR|date = 17 September 2010}} With St Helens choosing to play Huddersfield in other semi-final match, Wigan were left with an away trip to face Leeds again.{{cite news|title=St Helens opt for Huddersfield Giants in Club Call|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/st_helens/9012759.stm|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=BBC|date=19 September 2010}} With a 6–26 victory, it was Wigan this time who won, ending Leeds' three-year run as Super League champions, with the result placing Wigan into the Grand Final where they would face St Helens.{{cite news|last=Wilson|first=Andy|title=Wigan end Leeds' Super League reign to set up St Helens showdown|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/sep/25/leeds-wigan-super-league|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=The Guardian|date=25 September 2010}} Leuluai was announced in the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] preliminary squad for the [[2010 Rugby League Four Nations|2010 Four Nations]].{{cite news|title=Kiwis call Super League duo|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_6361890,00.html|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=6 September 2010}} [[Stuart Fielden]] and [[Andy Coley]] both signed new contracts to keep them at Wigan, for two years and one year respectively.{{cite news|title=Wigan's Stuart Fielden & Andy Coley sign new contracts|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/8976845.stm|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=BBC|date=7 September 2010}} Four Wigan players were announced in the [[Super League Dream Team]] for 2010: Richards, O'Loughlin and both of the Tomkins brothers.{{cite news|title=Wigan quartet in Dream Team|url=http://www.skysports.com/football/euro2008/story/0,23063,12196_6362493,00.html|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=7 September 2010}} Two days after Wigan had defeated Leeds for a place in the Grand Final, it was confirmed that [[Terry Newton]], the former Wigan player who had been banned in February for testing positive for [[Growth hormone|human growth hormone]] while playing for Wakefield, had been discovered dead at his home near Wigan. The death was later given an open verdict rather than one of suicide by the coroner, due to doubts over Newton's mental state at the time of his death.{{cite news|last=Wilson|first=Andy|title=Terry Newton's death 'may have been drugs warning to others' – coroner|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/dec/20/terry-newton-death-inquest|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=The Guardian|date=20 December 2010}} ===October=== {{see also|2010 Super League Grand Final}} 2010 was Wigan's fourth appearance in a Grand Final, three less than their opponents and local derby rivals St Helens, who held the record for most finals and were playing at [[Old Trafford]] for the fifth consecutive time having lost the previous three finals to Leeds.{{cite news|title=St Helens share Grand Final victory record|url=http://www.liverpoolecho.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/2010/10/02/st-helens-share-grand-final-victory-record-100252-27383072/|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=Liverpool Echo|date=2 October 2010}} Wigan's most previous final had been the defeat by Bradford in 2003, and O'Loughlin was the only survivor in 2010 from the team that played that day. Compared to St Helens' Grand Final record of four wins from seven, Wigan's record was poor, with their only Super League championship after the playoffs were introduced coming in 1998 against Leeds. With the league's statistically best defence in Wigan opposing the league's best attack in St Helens, and the top two teams from the regular season league table playing each other, the final was deemed by several commentators as a close match to call.{{cite news|title=Too close to call|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,11979_6418143,00.html|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=1 October 2010}}{{cite news|title=Final thoughts|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,11979_6409524,00.html|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=Sky Sports|date=1 October 2010}}{{cite news|last=Slater|first=Gary|title=Wigan Warriors 22 St Helens 10: match report|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8039045/Wigan-Warriors-22-St-Helens-10-match-report.html|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=The Daily Telegraph|date=2 October 2010}} The match was preceded with a minute's silence to Newton. Wigan earned the early advantage with the former St Helens player, Gleeson, scoring the game's opening try after three minutes following an [[Iosia Soliola]] knock-on.{{cite news|last=Lancaster |first=Rob |title=Warriors too good for Saints |url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3326090,00.html |access-date=16 July 2011 |newspaper=Sky Sports |date=2 October 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101005001030/http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0%2C19936%2C11070_3326090%2C00.html |archive-date=5 October 2010}} Deacon continued to put St Helens under pressure with threatening kicks to Richards, but it was Gleeson who scored his and Wigan's second try thanks to a move that started in the Warriors' half with Sam Tomkins and Deacon. Another Deacon kick was not properly controlled by St Helens, and Goulding touched down an easy try in the sixteenth minute. Richards scored his second goal with the conversion attempt, but did not last the first half as he suffered an injury to his Achilles tendon which ended his participation in the final. After coming close to a try through [[Matty Smith (rugby league)|Matty Smith]], St Helens eventually replied through [[Andrew Dixon (rugby league)|Andrew Dixon]] to reduce the margin to ten points at half-time. Richards' injury cost Wigan points, as Riddell, the stand-in kicker, missed two relatively easy penalty attempts. However, Sam Tomkins scored the first try of the second half on the right wing, with Riddell managing to successfully kick the conversion. [[Liam Farrell]] also had a try disallowed for obstruction, before St Helens scored their second try of the match through [[Francis Meli]]. It was the final try of the game though, as Wigan managed to run down the clock without conceding again to win their first Grand Final in twelve years. The 22–10 victory meant Wigan were crowned Super League XV champions. In addition to O'Loughlin and the Tomkins brothers who played against France earlier in the season, Fielden and Goulding were both selected to join the England squad in the post-season 2010 Four Nations tournament.{{cite news|last=Laybourne|first=Ian|title=Morley to captain England in Four Nations|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/morley-to-captain-england-in-four-nations/story-e6frfgbo-1225934087778|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=Herald Sun|date=5 October 2010}} It confirmed the day after the Grand Final that the injury Richards had sustained meant that he would be out of action for at least five to six months, which included the post-season international matches as well as the start of the next season, although Richards stated, "If I'd known that would happen and we'd still end up winning, I'd have taken it."{{cite news|title=Wigan's Pat Richards out with Achilles tendon tear|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/9055183.stm|accessdate=16 July 2011|newspaper=BBC|date=3 October 2010}} ==Fixtures== {| class="wikitable" border="1" ! colspan="2" | LEGEND |- | style="background:#ddffdd;" width=20 |   | width=100 | Win |- | style="background:#ffffdd" width=20 |   | width=100 | Draw |- | style="background:#ffdddd;" width=20 |   | width=100 | Loss |- |} ===Super League=== {{main|Super League XV}} {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:95%;" width="60%" !
